question: Determine if the following are perfect squares: 94
explanation: Let me test the neighbors. I know 9 times 9 gives 81, and 10 times 10 gives 100, so 94 sits snug between those two. Since it falls in the gap between one whole square and the next, no whole number multiplied by itself lands on it. So the answer is No.
hints: Think of the whole numbers whose squares crowd close around 94 — find the two that hem it in.
Start below: 9 times 9 is 81. That is the last square before we reach 94.
Now the one above: 10 times 10 is 100. So 81 < 94 < 100, and 94 rests in the gap with no whole number to claim it.
storyContext: By candlelight Stevin lays out 94 tokens for a paymaster who insists they can be packed into a perfect square array of men, each row matching each column. Stevin frowns at the count before him.

question: Solve 2( x + 1 )-3 = 5
explanation: Right, let me clear the clutter first — I add 3 to both sides, so 2(x + 1) comes to 8. Then I split that in half, giving x + 1 = 4, and finally I take away the 1 that's clinging on. That leaves me x = 3.
hints: Don't touch the bracket yet — peel away the loose numbers hanging outside it first, one at a time.
That lonely −3 is the first to go: add 3 to both sides and see what the left-hand side settles to.
Now you've got 2(x + 1) = 8. Halve both sides to reach x + 1 = 4, then knock off that last 1 — the answer's a step away.
storyContext: Below deck, William checks his father's watch against the mate's calculation of the day's run. The figures must balance: 2(x + 1) − 3 = 5, or the reckoning drifts and the coast turns to rocks.

question: Multiply (x + 3) (x + 1) .
explanation: See, I take each piece of the first bracket and lay it against each piece of the second. From x times x I get x^2, then x times 1 gives x, and 3 times x gives 3x, and last 3 times 1 gives 3. Now x and 3x are of one kind, so I gather them into 4x, and I am left with x^2 + 4x + 3.
hints: Every part of the first bracket must greet every part of the second — do not let any term go unmultiplied.
Begin with the leading terms of each bracket: x times x. Fix that piece first, then move outward.
You will have four products: x times x, x times 1, 3 times x, and 3 times 1. Write them all, then notice which two are alike and can be joined.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi sketches two adjoining plots of land, one measuring (x + 3) along its edge and the other (x + 1), and must find the full expanse they cover together.

question: Determine which of the following are rational numbers: √ 13
explanation: A rational number is one I can write as a ratio p/q of whole numbers, so first I ask whether √13 is a tidy whole number. But 3 times 3 gives 9 and 4 times 4 gives 16, so √13 sits between 3 and 4, never landing on a whole number. Since 13 has no square factor to pull out cleanly, no ratio of integers will ever equal it — the answer is none, √13 is irrational.
hints: Ask yourself the plain question: can this number be written as one whole number divided by another? That is the whole test.
Check first whether √13 is a neat whole number — try squaring the counting numbers near it and see if any land exactly on 13.
You will find 3 times 3 is 9 and 4 times 4 is 16, so √13 lies between 3 and 4 with no whole number to rest on; now consider whether such a value can be captured by any ratio of integers.
storyContext: By candlelight in Antwerp, Stevin measures a square plot whose area comes to 13 square rods, and the surveyor demands the exact length of its side before the deed can be signed.

question: Solve 6( y-2 )-5y = 4( y + 3 )-4( y - 1 ) .
explanation: I take the left side first: 6(y-2) is 6y - 12, and taking away 5y leaves me with y - 12. On the right, 4(y+3) is 4y + 12 and 4(y-1) is 4y - 4, so subtracting gives 4y + 12 - 4y + 4, which is just 16. That leaves y - 12 = 16, so y must be 28.
hints: Don't panic at all those brackets — clear them one at a time on each side and see what's left.
Start with the left: 6(y-2) - 5y. Multiply out the 6, then gather your y terms together.
On the right, 4(y+3) - 4(y-1) becomes 4y + 12 - 4y + 4 — the y terms cancel and you're left with a plain number. Set that equal to y - 12 and read off y.
storyContext: Below deck, William steadies his father's watch against the ship's roll and squints at the navigator's scrawled figures, a tangle of readings he must reconcile before the noon sighting.

question: Multiply (x - 3) (2x + 1) .
explanation: I take each piece of the first quantity and pair it with each piece of the second. From x times 2x I get 2x^2, and x times 1 gives x; then -3 times 2x gives -6x, and -3 times 1 gives -3. Gathering the middle terms, x and -6x join into -5x, so the area is 2x^2 - 5x - 3.
hints: Two quantities of two parts each — let every part of the first greet every part of the second, and leave none unmet.
Begin with the first part, x: multiply it by both 2x and by 1 before you move on.
You will have four terms: 2x^2 from x times 2x, x from x times 1, -6x from -3 times 2x, and -3 from -3 times 1. Now unite the two terms that carry x.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi sketches a plot of land whose length is (x - 3) cubits and whose width is (2x + 1). To record its area in his book, he must express the whole as a single expression.

question: Sam and Henry are roommates. Together, they have 68 books. Sam has 26 books. How many books does Henry have?
explanation: Right, we know the two of them together own 68 books, and Sam's already laid claim to 26 of that lot. So whatever's left over must be Henry's — I just take 68 and pull away the 26 that aren't his. That leaves me 42, and sure enough 42 and 26 back together make 68, so Henry has 42.
hints: Think of the whole shelf split between two men — if I know the total and one man's share, the rest belongs to the other.
Start from the full count of 68, since that's everything the two of them own together.
Take Sam's 26 away from the total of 68, and what remains is Henry's count.
storyContext: Below deck, William tallies the ship's small library shared by two officers. He knows the whole shelf holds 68 volumes, and one man claims 26 of them, but the other's count is missing from the ledger.

question: Factor: x^2 - 16x + 63 .
explanation: I look for two numbers that multiply to 63 and together make -16. Since the product is positive but the sum is negative, both numbers must be negative — so I test -7 and -9, and indeed (-7)(-9) = 63 while -7 + (-9) = -16. That gives me (x - 7)(x - 9).
hints: The whole matter turns on two numbers: one pair that multiplies to the last term and adds to the middle one. Find that pair and the work is nearly done.
Notice the last term is positive but the middle term is negative — this tells me both of my numbers share the same sign, and it is the negative one.
Now seek two negative numbers whose product is 63 and whose sum is -16; once you name them, simply place each with x inside its own parentheses.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi unrolls a plan for a rectangular courtyard whose area is written as x^2 - 16x + 63, and he must learn the two lengths hidden within it before the masons arrive at dawn.

question: After your trip to France, you have 180 Euros remaining. You wish to convert them back into U.S. dollars. Assuming the exchange rate is the same (\1 dollars =0.82 €) , how many dollars should you receive? Round to the nearest cent if necessary.
explanation: The merchant tells me each dollar is worth 0.82 Euros, so to learn how many dollars hide inside my 180 Euros, I must count how many times 0.82 fits into 180. I divide 180 ÷ 0.82 and get 219.5121951..., a number with too long a tail for any coin. Trimming it to the nearest cent, my purse should hold 219.51 dollars.
hints: The rate ties dollars and Euros together — think about which way to work it when you already hold the Euros and want the dollars.
Since 1 dollar equals 0.82 Euros, ask how many groups of 0.82 live inside your 180 Euros.
Set up 180 ÷ 0.82 and carry out the division, then round the long result to the nearest cent.
storyContext: Before Thales sailed home to Miletus, a Giza merchant offers to trade back the 180 Euros left in his pouch, muttering the day's rate as he weighs his coins.

question: On June 7, 2021, the national average price for regular gasoline was 3.053 dollars per gallon. If Aiko fills up his car with 16 gallons, how much is the total cost? Round to the nearest cent.
explanation: I take the price, 3.053, and stack it sixteen times over — easier if I split it. Ten gallons comes to 3.053 × 10 = 30.53, and six gallons is 3.053 × 6 = 18.318. Adding them gives 30.53 + 18.318 = 48.848, and since coin stops at the penny, that rounds to 48.85.
hints: Sixteen gallons at one price each — this is just the same number counted over and over. Multiplication saves the ink.
Break the sixteen into friendlier parts: figure 3.053 × 10 first, then handle the remaining 6 gallons.
You'll have 30.53 from the ten and 18.318 from the six; add them for 48.848, then trim to the nearest cent as coin demands.
storyContext: Below decks, William tallies the provisioning ledger by lantern light. The ship's mate needs the fuel cost reckoned before the chandler's boat departs at dawn, and the sum sits half-finished on the page.

question: A person who weighs 200 pounds on Earth would weigh only 33 pounds on the moon. A 2021 Toyota Prius weighs 3,040 pounds on Earth; how much would it weigh on the moon? Round to the nearest tenth if necessary.
explanation: See here — a thing that weighs 200 on Earth becomes only 33 upon the moon, so the moon claims a fraction of 33/200, which is 0.165 of any weight. I take the cart's Earth weight of 3040 and keep only that same fraction: 3040 × 0.165. Breaking it apart, 3040 × 0.1 is 304, 3040 × 0.06 is 182.4, and 3040 × 0.005 is 15.2 — summed, they give 501.6.
hints: Every weight shrinks by the same fraction on the moon; first find what fraction of the Earth weight remains.
Compare the two known weights: divide 33 by 200 to reveal the ratio the moon preserves.
You now hold the ratio 0.165 — multiply it by the cart's Earth weight of 3040 to learn its moon weight.
storyContext: As Thales waits in the shade, a young scribe recounts a tale of a distant silver moon where objects grow strangely light, and wonders aloud how much a merchant's laden cart would weigh upon it.

question: Write an application that can be solved using the equation 25x + 75 = 200 . Then solve your application.
explanation: The 75 is the flat fee, so I strip it off first: 200 − 75 leaves 125 shillings paid for crates alone. Each crate ran 25 shillings, so I split 125 into groups of 25 — that comes to 5. So x is 5 crates.
hints: The total lumps together one fixed charge and a run of equal charges — get the fixed part off the ledger before you worry about the crates.
Take the 75 off both sides so only the crate cost stands alone: 25x = 200 − 75.
Now you've got 25x = 125, so ask how many 25s fit into 125 — divide and read off x.
storyContext: William counts out coins below deck to pay the ship's chandler: a fixed harbor fee of 75 shillings plus 25 shillings for each crate of provisions loaded, and the ledger totals 200 shillings.

question: Solve (x - 2)(x + 5) = 0 .
explanation: See here: two quantities multiplied give zero, and that can only happen if one of them is itself zero. So I take x - 2 and let it be zero, which means x sits 2 above nothing, giving x = 2. Then I take x + 5 and let it be zero, which pulls x down to x = -5. Thus x = 2 or x = -5.
hints: When two things multiplied together come to nothing, ask yourself: what must be true of at least one of them?
Take the first piece, x - 2, and set it alone equal to zero. What must x be?
Now do the same with the second piece: x + 5 = 0. Move the 5 to the other side and read off what x becomes in each case.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi bends over a scroll where two lengths, written as (x - 2) and (x + 5), are multiplied together and marked equal to nothing at all.

question:
There are 16 math majors and 12 non-math majors in Ms. Kraft’s class. What is not a correct way to express the ratio of math majors to non-math majors?
16:12
12:16
4:3
explanation: A ratio names things in the order they are asked: math to non-math, so the first number must be the 16 and the second the 12, giving 16:12. If I divide both by 4, I get 4:3, which speaks the same truth in smaller terms. But 12:16 puts the non-math majors first — it reverses the order I was asked for, so that is the one that does not belong. The answer is 12:16.
hints: Notice which group is named first in the question — the order of a ratio is not idle; it decides which number leads.
Write the ratio exactly as asked: math majors first, then non-math majors, in that order.
You have 16:12 written correctly, and dividing both by 4 gives another true form. Now look at the choice that swaps which group comes first — that reversal is where the error hides.
storyContext: As the priests wait, Thales recalls the scribes counting apprentices in the temple school — 16 who study geometry, 12 who study other arts. To order his own thoughts, he must set down the proper comparison of the two groups.

question: Solve 3x-7-( x + 5 ) = 2( x-6 ).
explanation: I clear the parentheses first: -(x + 5) turns into -x - 5, so my left side reads 3x - 7 - x - 5, which tidies down to 2x - 12. The right side, 2(x - 6), is also 2x - 12 — the two sides are the very same expression. When both banks of the equation match exactly like that, any value of x holds true, so the answer is all real numbers.
hints: Don't rush to isolate x — first make each side as plain as you can and see what you're truly comparing.
Mind that minus sign in front of (x + 5): it flips both terms, giving -x - 5.
Once you combine the left into 2x - 12 and distribute the right into 2x - 12, set them beside each other and notice they are identical — now ask what that means for x.
storyContext: Below deck, William checks two ledgers of longitude reckoning against the watch's ticking heart, and both columns of figures seem to insist upon the very same truth no matter how he balances them.

question: Solve x^2 + 2x-15 = 0 .
explanation: See how the equation squares a number, adds twice that number, and leaves us short by 15. I look for two numbers that multiply to -15 yet add to 2 — the pair 5 and -3 does exactly this. So I write it as (x + 5)(x - 3) = 0, and since a product is zero only when a piece is zero, either x + 5 = 0 or x - 3 = 0, giving x = -5 or x = 3.
hints: Do not fear the square. Think of the equation as inviting you to split it into two simpler pieces multiplied together.
Hunt for two numbers whose product is -15 and whose sum is 2 — the -15 and the 2 are your two clues.
Once you find that pair, write the equation as (x + 5)(x - 3) = 0, then ask what each factor must equal for the whole product to vanish.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi bends over a scroll where a merchant's disputed accounts have collapsed into a single stubborn balance: x^2 + 2x - 15 = 0. The lamp gutters as he searches for the value that settles it.

question:
There are 16 math majors and 12 non-math majors in Ms. Kraft’s class. What shows the ratio of math majors to all the students in Ms. Kraft’s class?
16:12
12:16
16:28
28:12
None of these
explanation: A ratio to 'all the students' must count everyone first, so I gather the 16 who study math and the 12 who do not: together that is 28 in the class. Then I set the math majors against this whole, 16 to 28 — not 16 to 12, for 12 is only part, not the total. So the answer is 16:28.
hints: The question asks about math majors compared to ALL students, so first make sure you know how many 'all' truly is.
Add the two groups together — the ones who study math and the ones who do not — to find the size of the whole class.
Now place the math majors, 16, beside that total you found, writing it as 16 : (the sum of 16 and 12).
storyContext: As the priests wait, a young scribe beside Thales frets over a tally from her teacher Ms. Kraft: 16 who study number, 12 who do not, and a question about how the few compare to the whole gathering.

question:
The purchasing department for a chain of bookstores wants to make sure they’re buying the right types of books to put on the shelves, so they take a sample of 20 books that customers bought in the last five days and record the genres:
Nonfiction
Young Adult
Romance
Cooking
Young Adult
Young Adult
Thriller
Young Adult
Nonfiction
True Crime
Romance
Nonfiction
Thriller
True Crime
Romance
True Crime
Thriller
Romance
Young Adult
Young Adult
Create a bar graph to visualize these data.
choices: Nonfiction 3, Young Adult 5, Romance 4, Cooking 1, Thriller 3, True Crime 3
Nonfiction 0.15, Young Adult 0.30, Romance 0.20, Cooking 0.05, Thriller 0.15, True Crime 0.15
Nonfiction 3, Young Adult 6, Romance 4, Cooking 1, Thriller 3, True Crime 3
Nonfiction 15, Young Adult 30, Romance 20, Cooking 5, Thriller 15, True Crime 15
explanation: I go down the list once, marking a stroke for each genre as it appears — no leaping ahead. Young Adult recurs the most, at entries 2, 5, 6, 8, 19, and 20, giving 6; Romance shows 4; Nonfiction, Thriller, and True Crime each give 3; and Cooking appears but once, at 1. So the counts stand: Nonfiction 3, Young Adult 6, Romance 4, Cooking 1, Thriller 3, True Crime 3.
hints: A chaos of entries proves nothing until it is counted. Take each genre as its own category and tally how often it appears.
Begin at the top and make a single mark for each genre you meet — Nonfiction, then Young Adult, then Romance, and so on down all twenty.
Gather your six categories: Nonfiction, Young Adult, Romance, Cooking, Thriller, True Crime. Count each one's strokes, then let the height of every bar equal its tally.
storyContext: Between rounds, Florence sets aside her ledgers of the dying and turns to a smaller list — twenty book genres a colleague asks her to tally. Numbers are numbers, and she cannot bear to see them left in a heap.

question: Calculate 4/9 + 7/12 .
explanation: First I look at the bottoms, 9 and 12, and hunt for a number both will divide into cleanly: 9 is 3×3 and 12 is 2×2×3, so together they need 2×2×3×3, which is 36. Now 4/9 becomes 16/36 when I multiply top and bottom by 4, and 7/12 becomes 21/36 when I multiply by 3. Add the tops, 16 and 21, keep the 36 beneath, and the soldier's wage is 37/36 of a florin.
hints: These two fractions cannot be joined while their bottoms disagree; find a single denominator that both 9 and 12 will fit into evenly.
Break each bottom into its factors: 9 is 3×3, 12 is 2×2×3. Gather what you need for both and you have your common denominator.
With 36 beneath each, rewrite 4/9 as 16/36 and 7/12 as 21/36, then simply add the numerators over that shared 36.
storyContext: By guttering candlelight, Stevin tallies a paymaster's ledger where one soldier is owed 4/9 of a florin for labor and 7/12 more for a night watch, and the two portions must become one sum.

question: Solve the formula I = Prt for t . This formula is used to calculate simple interest I , for a principal P , invested at a rate r , for t years.
explanation: I've got I = Prt staring at me, and I need t standing alone. Right now t is tangled up with P and r, multiplied together, so I undo that by dividing both sides by Pr. On the right that clears everything but t, and on the left I'm left with I/(Pr). So t = I/(Pr).
hints: Think about what's clinging to t and how you'd peel it away — t is being multiplied by two things.
Whatever you do to one side you do to the other, so try dividing both sides by Pr.
After dividing by Pr, the right side collapses to just t while the left becomes I over Pr — write that quotient out.
storyContext: Below deck, William studies the interest ledger his father left to fund the voyage, knowing the total earned and the rate, yet needing to reckon how many years the sum has been growing.

question: Solve using the Square Root Property: x^2 = 25 .
explanation: I see the square of the unknown length equals 25, so the length itself must be a number whose square gives 25. Taking the root of both sides, I ask what number multiplied by itself yields 25 — and it is 5, since 5 times 5 makes 25. But do not forget: a negative multiplied by itself also becomes positive, so -5 times -5 is 25 as well. Thus x = 5, x = -5.
hints: Think of undoing the squaring — what operation reverses a number multiplied by itself?
Take the square root of both sides of x^2 = 25, and remember the root can be taken as positive or negative.
You have x = ±√25; now ask which number times itself makes 25, and hold onto both its positive and negative forms.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi bends over a surveyor's plan where a square plot must enclose an area of exactly 25 square measures, yet the scribe has left the length of its side unwritten.

question:
One U.S. dollar is worth 0.72 British pounds. Damon is traveling to Great Britain and wishes to exchange 450 dollars U.S. dollars for British pounds. How many British pounds should Damon get in return?
625
6,250
3,456
345.6
None of these
explanation: Let us follow the trade honestly: each single coin becomes 0.72 of the other, so we take 450 lots of 0.72. I find it easier to reckon 450 times 72, which gives 32400, then step the decimal back to divide by 100, leaving 324. Look now at the choices offered — 625, 6250, 3456, 345.6 — and 324 sits among none of them, so the answer is None of these.
hints: When one thing exchanges for a fixed portion of another, you gather that portion as many times as you have coins — think of it as repeated multiplication.
Set it up plainly: multiply the 450 coins by the rate 0.72, the pounds gained for each dollar.
Work 450 times 72 to get 32400, then shift for the two decimal places by dividing by 100 — compare your result against every listed choice before deciding.
storyContext: A merchant beside the pyramid frowns over his ledger, muttering that he must trade 450 of his foreign coins into the local currency at a rate of 0.72, yet he cannot trust his own reckoning.

question: Solve the formula V = 1/3π r^2 h for h . This formula is used to calculate the volume V of a right circular cone with radius r and height h .
explanation: I've got V = (1/3)*pi*r^2*h, and that pesky thirding is what's tangling me up, so I multiply both sides by 3 to clear it: 3V = pi*r^2*h. Now h is only trapped behind pi and r^2, so I divide both sides by pi*r^2 to set it free. That leaves h = 3V/(pi*r^2).
hints: The height h is buried under a fraction and two factors. Think about peeling those away one at a time until h stands alone.
That 1/3 out front is the first nuisance. Multiply both sides by 3 to be rid of it before anything else.
After multiplying by 3 you have 3V = pi*r^2*h. Now divide both sides by pi*r^2 and see what's left standing beside h.
storyContext: Below deck, William measures the brass funnel meant to steady a spare timekeeper in rough seas. He knows its volume and the width of its mouth, but must reckon its exact height before the ship's carpenter can cut a fitting.

question: Solve using the Square Root Property: 25p^2 = 49 .
explanation: Look here — I have 25p^2 = 49, so first I lift the 25 off by dividing both sides, leaving p^2 = 49/25. Now p is whatever number squares to 49/25, and since 7 gives 49 and 5 gives 25, the fraction 7/5 works. But a negative squares to a positive too, so both signs hold: p = 7/5, -7/5.
hints: The p is trapped under a squaring and a multiplier of 25 — free it one layer at a time before you reach for any root.
Divide both sides by 25 so p stands more alone: p^2 = 49/25.
Now take the square root of both sides, and remember a squared number hides two origins, one positive and one negative: p = ±√(49/25).
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi measures a bronze tile whose squared side, multiplied by 25, must equal 49 to fit the mosaic frame. He needs the true length of that side before the artisans cut.

question: The HO scale for model trains is the most common size of model trains. This scale is 1:87 . If a real locomotive is 73 feet long, how long should the model locomotive be (in inches)? Round your answer to the nearest inch.
explanation: First I take the true length, 73 feet, and speak it in inches, for 1 foot holds 12 of them: 73 × 12 = 876 inches. Then, since the little copy is 87 times smaller, I divide: 876 ÷ 87 = 10.07 and a whisper more. Rounding to the nearest whole inch, the model measures 10 inches.
hints: The scale 1:87 tells us the model is a shrunken echo of the real thing — but first make sure both are measured in the same unit before you shrink anything.
The real length is given in feet, yet we want inches. Turn 73 feet into inches by multiplying by 12.
Once you have 876 inches, apply the scale by dividing by 87, then round what you find to the nearest inch.
storyContext: As the priests wait, a merchant beside Thales unrolls a tiny carved likeness of a great sledge-locomotive from distant lands, boasting it shrinks the true beast 87 times over, and asks Thales its length in the small.

question: Solve using the quadratic formula: a^2 - 2a-15 = 0 .
explanation: I read the coefficients straight from the line: A = 1, B = -2, C = -15. The heart of the formula is the piece under the root, B^2 - 4AC, which becomes 4 - (-60) = 64, and the root of 64 is a clean 8. So a = (2 ± 8)/2, giving me (2 + 8)/2 and (2 - 8)/2, which settle at a = 5 or a = -3.
hints: Do not try to guess the factors by eye — lay the equation against the form Aa^2 + Ba + C = 0 and let the formula carry you.
Name your three coefficients first: A = 1, B = -2, C = -15. Everything else grows from those.
Build the discriminant B^2 - 4AC: that is 4 - (-60) = 64, whose root is 8, so drop it into (-B ± 8)/(2A) and read off both values.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi unrolls a surveyor's dispute over a rectangular garden whose measurements collapse into a single stubborn relation: a^2 - 2a - 15 = 0. The court waits for the length that satisfies it.

question: For each of the following situations, identify whether the sample is a simple random sample, a systematic random sample, a stratified random sample, a cluster random sample, or none of these. The chairperson of the University Chess Club is trying to decide on a time for the club’s regular meetings, so she emails all of the members of the club to find their preferences.
explanation: Look closely at what she actually did: she wrote to every single member, not to a chosen few. When you reach the whole population and leave no one out, you have not sampled at all — there is nothing to be random about. That makes this a census, and a census belongs to none of these sampling types.
hints: Before naming any method, ask the simplest question: did she pick out a portion of the members, or reach them all?
Notice the word 'all' — she emails every member of the club, not a selected group drawn from it.
If no subset is drawn and the entire membership is contacted, then no sampling has occurred — so consider which category such a case must fall under.
storyContext: Between rounds in the ward, a young orderly who runs the officers' chess club asks Florence how he ought to gather the men's views on when to meet, mentioning he simply wrote to every last member.

question: Rewrite 95/14 as an improper fraction.
explanation: See, I take the whole 9 and break each unit into fourteenths, so 9 times 14 gives me 126 fourteenths. Then I add the 5 fourteenths already written, and 126 + 5 comes to 131. The denominator stays 14, for I have counted everything in fourteenths, and so the figure stands as 131/14.
hints: A mixed number is just a whole and a part living together — think how many fourteenths hide inside that whole 9 before you count anything else.
Begin by multiplying the whole number by the denominator: 9 times 14. That tells you how many fourteenths the whole part is worth.
You have 126 fourteenths from the whole, now add the 5 fourteenths already given, and keep 14 beneath the line as your denominator.
storyContext: By candlelight, Simon tallies a ration ledger where a paymaster has scrawled 9 and 5/14 barrels against a single company, and the mixed figure must be recast into a single fraction before the accounting can proceed.

question: The average price of a gallon of regular gasoline in the California on July 1, 2021 was 4.28 dollars per gallon. Albert stops at a gas station in California and puts 9.5 gallons of gasoline into his Civic. How much did he pay for the gas?
explanation: Each measure costs 4.28, and he needs nine and a half of them, so I lay it out in two parts. Nine measures come to 4.28 times 9, which is 38.52, and the half measure is 4.28 times 0.5, which is 2.14. Set them together, 38.52 and 2.14, and the total rises to 40.66.
hints: When something has a single price and you want many of it, the counting turns into multiplying — one cost, repeated as many times as there are measures.
Begin with the whole measures alone: take 4.28 and multiply it by 9 before you worry about the leftover half.
You have 4.28 times 9 giving 38.52, and 4.28 times 0.5 giving 2.14 for the half measure — now only the joining of these two remains.
storyContext: The priests fall silent as a caravan merchant beside Thales grumbles about his costs, muttering that he must know the price of filling nine and a half measures of oil before he dares trade in Giza.

question: Round 5.1082 to three decimal places.
explanation: Watch with me: three decimal places means I keep through 5.108, and the fourth digit, the 2, decides whether that last 8 climbs or holds. Since 2 is less than 5, it is too small to nudge the 8 upward, so the 8 stays put and the tail simply falls away. That leaves us 5.108.
hints: The count of decimal places tells you where to draw your knife; look at what sits just past that mark to know whether the last kept digit rises.
Mark the third decimal place: in 5.108|2 the digit you are keeping is the 8, and everything after the line is what you must weigh.
The digit beyond the line is 2, and 2 is less than 5, so it cannot lift the 8 — leave the 8 unchanged and let the rest drop.
storyContext: Simon slides the ledger toward you. Break the whole into parts — every tenth counts.

question: Rewrite the following as fractions: 3%
explanation: The word percent simply whispers 'per hundred,' so when they say 3% they mean 3 parts drawn out of a whole of 100. I place the 3 above and the 100 below, giving 3/100. I check whether the two share any common divisor, but 3 is prime and does not divide 100, so nothing shrinks — the fraction is 3/100.
hints: Listen to the word itself — 'percent' carries its meaning in its sound. Ask what 'per hundred' is truly telling you.
Take the number 3 and imagine it as a piece of a whole divided into one hundred equal shares. Where does the 3 sit, and where does the 100 sit?
Write 3 as the top of the fraction and 100 as the bottom, then look for any number that divides both — if none exists, the fraction already stands in its simplest form.
storyContext: A priest murmurs that the sacred stones weathered by only 3% each century, and Thales, tracing the number in the sand, must render that portion in the honest language of fractions.

question: Round 18.6298 to two decimal places.
explanation: I want only two figures past the point, so I mark the cut: 18.62|98. Now I peer at the next figure, the 9 that follows the 2. A 9 is well past halfway, so it pushes the 2 upward to a 3, and the tail falls away. The wage settles at 18.63.
hints: Decide first how many figures you mean to keep past the point, then let the very next figure be your judge.
Draw a line after the second decimal place: 18.62|98. The digit just to the right of that line tells you what to do.
That third figure is 9, and any figure of 5 or more nudges the one before it upward — so the 2 must climb, and the rest is discarded.
storyContext: By candlelight, Stevin tallies a canal-worker's daily wage and lands on 18.6298 guilders, but the coin chest holds no piece smaller than a hundredth of a guilder.

question: Convert the following percents to decimal form: 9%
explanation: Look here, a percent is only a way of speaking about parts of one hundred, so 9% means 9 out of 100, or 9/100. When I divide 9 by 100, each digit slides two places to the right, past the point. That leaves me with 0.09.
hints: Remember what the word percent whispers to us: it always speaks of a portion out of one hundred.
Set the number over one hundred, so that 9% becomes 9/100.
Now divide 9 by 100 — move the digit two places past the point and read what remains.
storyContext: A priest scratches a figure into the sand: the shadow reaches 9% beyond a marked stone. Thales murmurs that such a fraction of a hundred must first be rendered plain before any measure can follow.

question: Create a binned frequency distribution to summarize these data.
choices: Cannot be determined — no data provided
Classes measured in decimals like 0.1 to 0.5
explanation: I reach for the numbers to sort them into bins, but the page before me holds none — only the instruction to summarize 'these data.' Without a single figure, I cannot choose class widths, count frequencies, or draw a single boundary. A distribution built from nothing would be a lie, and I do not deal in lies. So the honest verdict is: cannot be determined — no data provided.
hints: Before you can bin anything, ask what raw values you have been handed to bin.
Look carefully at the problem statement for the actual list of numbers — the deaths, the counts, any figures at all.
If you search every line and find no numbers to sort into ranges, then there is nothing to distribute — and that absence itself is your finding.
storyContext: Florence sits before a mountain of scribbled ward records at Scutari, resolved to sort the deaths into ranges so the pattern of dying might finally show itself.

question: For the following exercises, multiply the binomials. (w + 5)(w + 7)
explanation: I take the whole first side, w and 5, and let each part reach across both parts of the second side. So w meets w to give w^2, and w meets 7 to give 7w; then 5 meets w for 5w, and 5 meets 7 for 35. The two middle pieces, 7w and 5w, are alike, so I gather them into 12w, and the area stands as w^2 + 12w + 35.
hints: Think of it as a rectangle: every part of the first side must touch every part of the second. Nothing may be left unpaired.
Begin with the leading terms — let w from the first factor multiply w from the second — and keep going through each pairing carefully.
You will have four products: w^2, 7w, 5w, and 35. The two terms that both carry a w are of the same kind — join them, and write down what remains.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi measures a rectangular plot for a scholar's garden. One side runs w plus 5 cubits, the other w plus 7, and the total area must be written as a single expression.

question: Convert the following to percent: 0.41
explanation: Look here — a decimal like 0.41 already tells us parts out of a whole, and percent simply means those parts counted per hundred. So I slide the point two places by multiplying by 100: 0.41 × 100 becomes 41. I mark it with the percent sign, and there it stands: 41%.
hints: Remember that 'percent' means nothing more than counting per hundred — so ask how many hundredths this decimal holds.
Take the decimal and multiply it by 100; that shift is the whole trick.
Compute 0.41 × 100, which moves the point two places to the right, then attach the percent sign to whatever number you find.
storyContext: Thales crouches in the sand, tracing his measurements. A priest reads from a papyrus that the shadow spans 0.41 of the pyramid's base, and Thales must render that fraction in the priests' own reckoning of hundredths.

question: Convert 17.03347 to fraction form.
explanation: See here, I count the figures after the point: 0, 3, 3, 4, 7 — five in all. So my denominator must be 10 multiplied five times, which is 100000. Now I lift the whole number onto that same footing: 17.03347 times 100000 slides the point across to give 1703347. I check whether it may be reduced — 100000 is built of 2 and 5 alone, yet 1703347 is odd, so no shared factor hides. Thus the length is 1703347/100000.
hints: Count how many figures trail behind the decimal point — that tally tells you which power of 10 to summon as your denominator.
There are five digits after the point, so let your denominator be 10 multiplied by itself five times: 100000.
Place 100000 beneath, and for the top, shift the point five places rightward through 17.03347 to form the whole numerator over 100000.
storyContext: By candlelight, Stevin marks a measured length of canal wall as 17.03347 rods, but the stonemasons demand it written as a proper fraction before they will cut a single block.

question: For the following exercises, factor the trinomials. a^2 - 5a - 14
explanation: I look at the expression a^2 - 5a - 14 and ask myself which two numbers multiply to give -14 yet add to give -5. I test -7 and 2: their product is -7 times 2, which is -14, and their sum is -7 plus 2, which is -5 — both conditions satisfied. So the parcel's sides are (a - 7)(a + 2).
hints: Look at the last number and the middle number together — the two hidden values must both multiply to one and add to the other.
First find every pair of numbers whose product is -14; since it is negative, one number must be positive and the other negative.
Among those pairs, seek the one that also adds to -5, then write your answer as (a + first)(a + second).
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi unrolls a merchant's ledger where a plot of land is described only by the expression a^2 - 5a - 14. To divide the parcel fairly, he must find the two side-lengths hidden within it.

question: For the following exercises, solve the linear equations using a general strategy. - (t - 19) = 28
explanation: First I clear that leading minus by pushing it across the parentheses, so -(t - 19) becomes -t + 19 = 28. Then I strip the 19 off the left, subtracting it from both sides to get -t = 9. Since I've got -t and not t, I flip both signs, and t comes out as -9.
hints: That minus sign hanging out front of the parentheses is the whole trick — deal with it before anything else.
Spread the negative across the inside: -(t - 19) turns into -t + 19, and that whole thing still equals 28.
Now clear the 19 from the left so you're left with -t = 9, then remember you want t, not -t — one flip of signs finishes it.
storyContext: Below deck, William checks his father's watch against the ship's log, where a smudged figure reads -(t - 19) = 28 and must equal the temperature drift the pendulum will bear.

question: What is the total if 18% of the total is 45?
explanation: Here is my thinking: 45 stands for 18% of the whole, so 0.18 times the total gives 45. That means the total is 45 divided by 0.18, which I turn into 4500 divided by 18 to be rid of the decimal. Working that quotient through, I find it lands squarely on 250 — and indeed 0.18 times 250 returns my 45, so the total is 250.
hints: When a piece and its portion are known, remember that the whole is what the portion is measured against — reach for that hidden total.
Write it as a balance: 0.18 times the total equals 45. The total is the thing you do not yet hold.
Free yourself of the decimal — 45 divided by 0.18 is the same as 4500 divided by 18. Carry out that division and the whole reveals itself.
storyContext: A priest presses further: he has counted 45 laborers who returned from the quarry, only 18% of those he first sent. Thales must know how many marched out at dawn if his tally is to mean anything.

question: Calculate 45.63 ÷ 17.13 using a calculator. Round to three decimal places, if necessary.
explanation: I lay 45.63 against 17.13 and ask how many times the smaller fits the larger. Two lengths of 17.13 make 34.26, leaving 11.37 over, which is a bit more than half of 17.13 again, near 0.6 of it. Carrying the reckoning further with care, the quotient settles at 2.664.
hints: Think of it as fitting the smaller length into the larger one, then measuring what scrap remains.
Begin by seeing how many whole 17.13's sit inside 45.63 — two of them, since 17.13 times 2 is 34.26.
After the two whole parts you have 11.37 left; divide that remainder by 17.13 to find the fraction beyond 2, then keep the figures to three places past the point.
storyContext: By candlelight, Stevin measures a stretch of new canal, 45.63 rods long, against a section his crew finished today, 17.13 rods. He must know how many of the shorter runs fit into the whole before dawn's work resumes.

question: For the following exercises, solve the linear equations using properties of equations. - 12 + 8(x - 5) = - 4 + 3(5x - 2)
explanation: Trace the path: Expand left side: 8(x−5) = 8x − 40. Left side becomes −12 + 8x − 40. Combine constants: −12 − 40 = −52. Left side = 8x − 52. Expand right side: 3(5x−2) = 15x − 6. That lands on -6 — the answer.
hints: The brackets are hiding the truth — clear them on both sides before you try to match anything.
Start on the left: 8(x−5) becomes 8x−40, and then fold in that −12 so the whole left reads as 8x minus a single number.
Once both sides are tidy you'll face 8x−52 = 15x−10; gather the x's on one side and the plain numbers on the other, then divide out what's left.
storyContext: Below deck, William balances the watch against the ship's roll, checking his father's tables where two rival timekeeping figures must be reconciled before the reading can be trusted.

question: Find the percent in the following: Total is 40, percent of the total is 25
explanation: See here, 25 percent means 25 out of every 100, so I write it as the fraction 25/100, which is simply 0.25. Now I take that portion of the whole 40, multiplying 0.25 by 40, and the sand gives me 10. To be sure, I divide 10 back into 40 and find 0.25 again, exactly a quarter, so the answer is 10.
hints: The word percent hides a fraction of the whole 40 — think of it as a portion out of one hundred.
First turn 25 percent into a decimal by dividing 25 by 100.
Now take that decimal and multiply it against the total, 0.25 times 40, and read what the sand shows you.
storyContext: Thales counts out 40 paces of shadow across the sand, and the priests demand he mark the stretch that lies within the 25 part they have measured off. He kneels, tracing the ratio in the dust.

question: For the following exercises, construct a linear equation to solve an application. It costs 0.55 dollars to mail one first class letter. Construct a linear equation and solve to find how much it costs to mail 13 letters.
explanation: Each letter costs 0.55 to mail, so I write it as C = 0.55n, with n the count of letters. With 13 in my hand, that's 0.55 times 13. I split it: 0.55 times 10 is 5.50, and 0.55 times 3 is 1.65, which add to 7.15.
hints: Every letter costs the same, so think of the total as that single price repeated for each one in the bundle.
Set up C = 0.55n, where n is how many letters I'm posting — here that's 13.
Put the numbers in: C = 0.55 times 13. Break it into 0.55 times 10 plus 0.55 times 3 and add the two pieces together.
storyContext: Below deck, William must post 13 sealed letters at the next port, each carrying word of the watch's progress home to his father. The dockside clerk demands to know the full postage before he'll take the bundle.

question: For the following exercises, solve the quadratic equation using the square root method. x^2 = 16
explanation: I have a number that, when multiplied by itself, gives 16, so I ask what side length produces that area. Taking the root of both sides, x equals the root of 16, and since 4 times 4 is 16, that works. But watch closely: a negative multiplied by itself is also positive, so -4 times -4 is 16 as well. Both belong here, so x = 4, -4.
hints: Think of 16 as the area of a square — what you seek is the side that builds it. The root is the tool for undoing a square.
Take the root of both sides so that x stands alone facing the root of 16.
Ask which number times itself gives 16, and remember that a value and its opposite both square to the same positive amount — so keep both the positive and the negative possibility.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi measures a square courtyard tile whose area the builders have recorded as 16 square handspans, and he must recover the length of its side before the masons continue.

question: In the following exercises, rewrite the percent as a fraction 45%
explanation: A percent is nothing more than a part out of one hundred, so 45% is simply 45/100. But that looks clumsy, so I search for what evenly divides both numbers, and I find 5 fits into each. Dividing top and bottom by 5 gives me 9 over 20 — the fraction is 9/20.
hints: Remember that the word percent means 'out of one hundred.' Let that guide how you first write the number.
Set the 45 above and 100 below, as a fraction, then look for a number that divides into both.
Both 45 and 100 share a common factor of 5. Divide the numerator by 5 and the denominator by 5, and the tidy fraction reveals itself.
storyContext: A priest crouches in the sand and marks that the shadow reaches 45% of the full stone base. Thales knows this ratio must become a clean fraction before his geometry can speak.

question: For the following exercises, data are collected on a sample of items found in a grocery store. Classify each of these datasets obtained from that sample as being categorical or quantitative. Price
explanation: Look here — Price is what a thing costs, and it comes to us as a number. I can add prices together, I can take an average across many items, and that arithmetic tells me something true. When numbers behave like numbers and their sums mean something, the data is Quantitative.
hints: Ask yourself the same question I ask of every figure in my ledgers: is this a label that names a thing, or a number that measures it?
Consider what Price actually is — the cost of an item. See whether it arrives as a word describing a category or as a value you could write down as an amount.
Test it as I would: try adding two prices together, or finding their average. If that arithmetic makes honest sense, you know which of the two kinds of data you are holding.
storyContext: Between wards, Florence steadies herself over a supply merchant's ledger, sorting each column of grocery figures before she dares organize her own mountain of death records.

question: Correctly apply the rules for the order of operations to accurately compute {( {3/16 + 7/16} )^2} + 1/5 ÷ 3/10 .
explanation: First I settle what sits inside the parentheses: 3/16 + 7/16 gives 10/16, which is simply 5/8. That squared is 25/64. Then the division, 1/5 ÷ 3/10, I turn into 1/5 × 10/3 = 10/15 = 2/3. Now I add 25/64 + 2/3 over a common bottom of 192: 75/192 + 128/192, and there it stands, 203/192.
hints: Mind the ordering as a good clerk must: settle what lies within the parentheses first, then the raising to a power and the division, and only at the last do you add.
Begin inside the brackets, for 3/16 and 7/16 share the same denominator and join cleanly into a single fraction you may reduce.
You have 25/64 from the squared sum, and 1/5 ÷ 3/10 becomes 1/5 × 10/3 = 2/3; now cast both over a common bottom of 192 and add the two together.
storyContext: By candlelight, Simon tallies a paymaster's ledger where two fractions of a guilder must be joined, their sum multiplied upon itself, then a further portion parceled out among the ranks.

question: For the following exercises, construct an application from a linear equation. 1,000x + 2,500 = 16,500 .
explanation: I start with 1,000x + 2,500 = 16,500, and that 2,500 is the harbor fee riding on top of everything, so I strip it off both sides and I'm left with 1,000x = 14,000. Now each crate costs 1,000, so I split that 14,000 into groups of a thousand. That gives me x = 14.
hints: That fixed harbor fee is clinging to the crate cost — think about how you'd peel it away before counting the crates.
Take 2,500 from both sides of the ledger so only the crate term stands alone on the left.
You've got 1,000x = 14,000; now share that 14,000 out evenly by the 1,000 each crate costs.
storyContext: Below deck, William tallies the voyage costs by lamplight. The provisioning ledger lists a fixed harbor fee of 2,500 shillings plus 1,000 per crate, and the master demands to know how many crates fit a total of 16,500.

question: For the following exercises, solve the quadratic equation using the quadratic formula. 16p^2 = 24p - 9
explanation: First I gather everything to one side so it rests at zero: 16p² - 24p + 9 = 0, giving me a = 16, b = -24, c = 9. Now I test the heart of the formula, b² - 4ac: (-24)² is 576, and 4·16·9 is also 576, so their difference is 0. When that inner part is nothing, the two roots fold into one, and (24 ± 0)/(2·16) = 24/32, which simplifies to 3/4.
hints: Before reaching for the formula, bring every term to one side so the equation faces zero — only then can you read off a, b, and c cleanly.
With 16p² - 24p + 9 = 0, name your coefficients: a = 16, b = -24, c = 9, and begin with the piece under the root, b² - 4ac.
Notice b² = 576 and 4ac = 576, so their difference vanishes — the root of that is 0, leaving you just (24 ± 0)/(2·16) to simplify.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi bends over a merchant's ledger where a balance of gold has been written as 16p² = 24p - 9, and he must find the single measure of p that keeps the account true.

question: In the following exercises, rewrite the percent in decimal form. 18%
explanation: When they say 18%, they mean 18 for every hundred, so I write it as 18/100. Dividing by a hundred simply slides the count two places down, past the units and into the smaller parts. So 18/100 becomes 0.18.
hints: Remember what the word 'percent' whispers: it counts things out of one hundred. Hold that meaning in your hand.
Set the number over a hundred first, as a fraction: 18/100.
Now divide 18 by 100 — dividing by a hundred shifts every digit two places toward the smaller values.
storyContext: A priest recalls that only 18% of last season's harvest was set aside for the temple, and he mutters the figure as a fraction of the whole. Thales listens, needing to render it in the language of his own reckoning.

question: For the following exercises, decide whether random samples are being selected. If they are, decide whether they are simple, systematic, cluster, or stratified. A newspaper asks its readers to answer an online poll about proposed zoning changes in their city.
explanation: Trace the path: Identify how respondents are chosen: readers voluntarily choose to answer. Check for randomness: no random selection mechanism is used. Since participants self-select, this is a voluntary (self-selected) sample. Therefore it is not a random sample. That lands on Not a random sample (voluntary response) — the answer.
hints: Before naming a type, ask the plainest question: how did each person come to be counted? That single answer tells you almost everything.
Notice that the readers themselves decide whether to answer the poll; no clerk, no lot, no chance procedure reaches out to pick them.
Since the respondents self-select and no random device chooses who speaks, weigh whether this can be any of the true random forms at all, or something else entirely.
storyContext: By lamplight Florence reviews a London newspaper that boasts of gathering the public's mind on hospital reform, inviting any reader who wishes to reply. She must judge whether such a gathering can be trusted like her own careful counts.

question: The product of two consecutive odd integers is 99. Find the integers.
explanation: I call the smaller integer n, so its neighbor two steps on is n+2, and their product must be 99. That gives me n^2 + 2n = 99, which I rearrange as n^2 + 2n - 99 = 0. Now I hunt two numbers that multiply to -99 and add to 2 — those are 11 and -9 — so n is 9 or -11, and the consecutive pair is 9 and 11, or -11 and -9.
hints: Do not chase the two numbers directly — give the first one a name, and let its odd neighbor be described in terms of it.
If the smaller is n, the next odd integer is n+2; write down that their product equals 99.
Expand n(n+2) = 99 into n^2 + 2n - 99 = 0, then look for two numbers that multiply to -99 and add to 2.
storyContext: A merchant sets a rug of tiles before al-Khwarizmi in the House of Wisdom, its two side lengths odd numbers just two apart, its full area a stubborn 99. He must know the sides before the sale can close.

question: For the following exercises, solve the given formula for the specified variable. Solve the formula C = π d for d .
explanation: I've got C = pi d, but it's the width d I'm after, not the way round. Since pi is just multiplying d, I undo that by dividing both sides by pi, and on the right the pi cancels clean out, leaving d alone. That gives me d = C/pi.
hints: The variable d isn't by itself yet — think about what's clinging to it and how you'd peel that away.
On the right side, d is being multiplied by pi. The opposite of multiplying is dividing.
Divide both sides by pi, so you've got C/pi on the left and pi d/pi on the right — now watch what happens to the pi beside d.
storyContext: William measures the great cabin's brass porthole, its rim worn smooth by salt. He knows the distance around it, C, but the shipwright wants the straight span across the glass to fit a new cover.

question: The product of two consecutive even integers is 168. Find the integers.
explanation: Let me name the smaller measure x, so its neighbor is x+2, and their product gives x(x+2) = 168. Spreading this out I have x^2 + 2x = 168, which I set to rest as x^2 + 2x - 168 = 0. Now I seek two numbers that multiply to -168 and add to 2, and 14 with -12 obey both. So x is either 12 or -14, giving the pairs 12 and 14, or -14 and -12.
hints: Do not guess the numbers blindly — give the first even integer a name, and let its companion be written in terms of that same name.
Call the smaller integer x; then the next even integer is x+2, and their product is written as x(x+2) = 168.
Open the product into x^2 + 2x = 168, then gather everything to one side as x^2 + 2x - 168 = 0, and hunt for two numbers that multiply to -168 while adding to 2.
storyContext: In the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi unrolls a merchant's ledger noting that a rectangular grain store's dimensions are two consecutive even measures whose product the scribe recorded as 168, yet the measures themselves were smudged away.

question: Ashley wants to study for 10 hours over the weekend. She plans to spend half the time studying math, a quarter of the time studying history, an eighth of the time studying writing, and the remaining eighth of the time studying physics. How much time will Ashley spend on each of those subjects?
choices: Math 50 hours, History 25 hours, Writing 12.5 hours, Physics 12.5 hours
Math 5 hours, History 2 hours, Writing 1 hour, Physics 1 hour
Math 5 hours, History 2.5 hours, Writing 1.25 hours, Physics 1.25 hours
Math 5 hours, History 2.5 hours, Writing 1.5 hours, Physics 1.5 hours
explanation: See here, ten hours is our whole. Half of ten is 5 for the math, and a quarter is 5 again halved, so 2.5 for history. An eighth of ten is 1.25, and that goes to writing, with another eighth of 1.25 for physics. Add them back: 5 + 2.5 + 1.25 + 1.25 makes 10, so all is accounted for — Math 5 hours, History 2.5 hours, Writing 1.25 hours, Physics 1.25 hours.
hints: Think of the ten hours as one full cask to be poured into four cups, each fraction telling how much of the whole it takes.
Begin with the largest share: take half of the ten hours and set that portion aside for the math.
Now find a quarter of ten for history, then an eighth of ten for writing and the same eighth again for physics; when you sum every part it must return to ten.
storyContext: By candlelight, Stevin overhears the paymaster's daughter fretting over her weekend ledger of study, ten hours to be split among four subjects by halves and eighths.

question: A rectangular patio has an area of 180 square feet. The width of the patio is three feet less than the length. Find the length and width of the patio.
explanation: Trace the path: Let length = L, width = L − 3. Area equation: L(L − 3) = 180. Expand: L² − 3L = 180. Rearrange: L² − 3L − 180 = 0. Find factors of −180 that sum to −3: −15 and 12. That lands on length = 15 feet, width = 12 feet — the answer.
hints: Name the length with a single unknown, and let the width lean upon it — for it is three feet less than that same length.
Since area is length times width, write L times (L − 3) equal to 180, then open the parentheses.
Bring all to one side to form L² − 3L − 180 = 0, then hunt for two numbers that multiply to −180 and add to −3 — the positive root among them is your length.
storyContext: In the courtyard of the House of Wisdom, al-Khwarizmi kneels over a scholar's request: a rectangular patio must cover 180 square feet, its width falling three feet short of its length, yet no one has named the sides.

question: Danny, a nutritionist, is designing a diet for her client, Callum. Danny determines that Callum’s diet should be 30% protein. If Callum consumes 2,400 calories per day, how many calories of protein should Danny tell Callum to consume?
explanation: See here, a part in the hundred is nothing to fear: 30% is simply 30 out of every 100, which I write as 0.30. Now I take the whole ration, 2400, and portion off that share: 2400 times 0.30. Reckon it plainly and you land upon 720 calories of protein.
hints: A percent is only a part measured against a hundred — turn that share into a plain fraction of the whole before you touch the total.
First tame the 30%: divide it by 100 so it becomes 0.30, a number you can multiply with ease.
Now set the whole against the share: multiply 2400 by 0.30, and the count of protein calories will fall out of the reckoning.
storyContext: By candlelight in his Antwerp study, Stevin sets aside the paymaster's ledgers to help an apothecary who must portion a soldier's daily ration, where 30% of the strength must come from meat.

question: In the following exercises, solve each linear equation. 15( y - 9 ) = - 60
explanation: I look at that 15 clinging to the whole (y - 9), so I split both sides by 15 to shake it loose — that leaves y - 9 on the left and -60/15, which comes to -4, on the right. Now it just reads y - 9 = -4, so I add 9 back to both sides to free the y, and -4 plus 9 gives me 5. So y is 5.
hints: That 15 is wrapped around the whole parenthesis — deal with it first before you fuss over the 9 inside.
Divide both sides by 15 and see what -60/15 becomes; that clears the multiplier away.
Once it reads y - 9 = -4, just add 9 to both sides and the value of y falls right out.
storyContext: William checks the watch against the ship's chronometer log, where the navigator scrawled 15(y - 9) = -60 to reckon a drift error in the day's readings. He must find y before the captain calls for the noon fix.

question: One mile is equal to 1.60934 km. Convert 200 miles to kilometers. Round off the answer to three decimal places.
explanation: Each mile is 1.60934 km, so 200 of them means I simply take 1.60934 and multiply by 200. I break it apart: 1.60934 times 2 gives 3.21868, and then times 100 shifts it up to 321.868. Kept to three places, the road runs 321.868 km.
hints: Every mile carries the same fixed length in kilometers — so 200 of them is just that length taken 200 times over.
Begin by multiplying 1.60934 by 2; the extra 100 can wait its turn.
You have 1.60934 × 2 = 3.21868; now let the 100 do its work and slide the point across.
storyContext: A Spanish messenger drops a war map on Stevin's table, its supply route marked in English miles. The prince's quartermaster waits, needing the distance in the leagues and kilometers his own men understand.

question: If 40% of the total is 32, how much is the total?
explanation: See, I know that 40% of the full length equals 32, so I write 0.40 times the total is 32. To find the whole, I simply undo the multiplication and divide 32 by 0.40. That gives me 32 ÷ 0.40 = 80, and to be sure I check: 0.40 × 80 is indeed 32. The total is 80.
hints: A part is known and its portion is known — think of how the whole relates to that fraction of it.
Set it down as I would: 0.40 × total = 32. Now what single operation frees the total?

question: If 150% of the total is 61.9, how much is the total?
explanation: See, 150% is just one and a half of the whole — so 1.5 times the total gives us 61.9. To find the total, I simply undo that: I divide 61.9 by 1.5. Working it out, 61.9 ÷ 1.5 lands at 41.2666..., and when I multiply 1.5 back onto it, 61.9 returns to me — so the total is 41.2666...

Write 30% as the number 0.30, then notice that 0.30 times the unknown total gives you 27.
Since 0.30 times the total equals 27, undo the multiplication by dividing 27 by 0.30 — carry out that division to find the whole.
storyContext: As the priests wait for Thales to measure the impossible, he recalls a scribe's riddle from the night before: a gathering of 27 apprentices was said to be but 30% of the temple's students, yet no one knew their true number.

question: Calculate the discount for the given original price and discount percentage. Then calculate the sale price. Original price = 1,550.00 dollars; percent discount is 32%
explanation: Trace the path: Convert percent to decimal: 32% = 0.32. Multiply original price by decimal: 1550.00 × 0.32. Compute 1550 × 0.32 = 496.00. Discount = 496.00. Subtract discount from original: 1550.00 − 496.00. That lands on Discount = 496.00, Sale price = 1054.00 — the answer.
hints: A percent is only a part of the whole — think of the discount as a slice cut from the full price before you worry about what is left.
Rewrite the 32% as a decimal, 0.32, so you have something you can multiply against the 1550.00.
Multiply 1550.00 × 0.32 to find the discount, then take that result away from 1550.00 to see what you still owe.
storyContext: A merchant beside the pyramid offers Thales fine linen priced at 1,550.00 dollars, then leans close and promises to shave 32% from that sum for a curious foreigner.

question: Determine the percent discount based on the given original and sale prices. Original price = 250.00 dollars; sale price = 162.50 dollars
explanation: First I see what was taken off: 250.00 − 162.50 = 87.50, the coins the merchant no longer asks for. Then I set that saving against the whole price, 87.50 / 250.00 = 0.35, for a part is only meaningful beside its whole. Turning that fraction into the language of the hundred, 0.35 becomes 35%.
hints: A discount is nothing but a part measured against the whole price. Find that part first, then weigh it against the original.
Begin by taking the sale price away from the original: 250.00 − 162.50. That difference is what the buyer keeps.
Now lay that difference over the whole: (250.00 − 162.50) / 250.00, then carry the result into hundredths to speak it as a percent.
storyContext: As Thales waits, a merchant in the shade offers him linen once priced at 250.00 dollars, now marked down to 162.50 dollars, and asks the traveler to name how deep the cut truly runs.

question: Calculate 3/8 + 5/12 and express in lowest terms.
explanation: These two parts cannot be joined while they wear different clothing, so I find a common measure for eighths and twelfths — 24 serves both. Then 3/8 becomes 9/24, and 5/12 becomes 10/24, and now they add cleanly: 9 and 10 make 19 parts of 24. Since 19 is prime and shares nothing with 24, it stands as it is: 19/24.
hints: You cannot add two parts measured against different wholes — first make them speak the same tongue by finding a size of piece that fits both eighths and twelfths.
Break each down: 8 is 2·2·2 and 12 is 2·2·3, so the smallest whole that holds both is 24. Rewrite each fraction over 24.
Now 3/8 = 9/24 and 5/12 = 10/24; add only the numerators over the shared 24, then check whether the result can be reduced any further.
storyContext: By candlelight, a paymaster slides two scraps across Stevin's table: one soldier owed 3/8 of a guilder for labor, another 5/12 for cartage, and the ledger demands a single sum.

question: Determine the original price based on the percent discount and sale price. Percent discount 15%; sale price = 11.05 dollars
explanation: The discount takes away 15%, so what remains is 85% of the first price, and that remainder equals 11.05. If 0.85 times the original gives 11.05, then the original is 11.05 divided by 0.85. Writing it as whole numbers, 1105 / 85, and since 85 times 13 lands exactly on 1105, the price before the discount was 13.
hints: The sale price is not the whole; it is only the part that survives after the discount is removed. Think about what fraction of the original still stands.
Taking away 15% leaves 85%, which as a decimal is 0.85. The 11.05 you see is that 0.85 of the price you seek.
So 0.85 times the original equals 11.05. Turn it around: the original is 11.05 / 0.85, which is the same as 1105 / 85. Now ask how many times 85 fits into 1105.
storyContext: A merchant in the shadow of the pyramid offers Thales a bronze measuring tool at 11.05 dollars, boasting it is marked down 15% from its true worth. Before he trades, Thales wishes to know the price it bore before the discount.

question:
A survey of college students asked how many courses those students were currently taking. The results are summarized in this frequency distribution:

Find the mode of the number of classes.
explanation: The mode is simply whichever count appears most frequently, so I run my finger down the frequency column: 1, 3, 16, 8, 4. The largest of these is 16, and it sits beside the row for 3 classes. Whatever occurs most often is the mode, and here that is 3.
hints: The mode is the value that shows up most, so let the frequency column be your guide — I look for where the numbers pile highest.
Read down the frequencies alone: 1, 3, 16, 8, 4. Which of these is the greatest?
The greatest frequency is 16 — now simply see which number of classes sits beside that row, and that is your mode.
storyContext: By lamplight Florence sorts the ward's tangled records, tallying how many treatments each soldier bears at once, hunting for the single value that repeats most often amid the chaos.

question: Convert 0.34 into fraction form.
explanation: The digits 34 sit two places past the point, so I read them as 34/100 — thirty-four parts of a hundred. Now 34 and 100 share a common factor of 2, and casting that out from both leaves 17 over 50, which no longer divides. So 0.34 stands as 17/50.
hints: A decimal is only a fraction wearing different clothes — count the places after the point and you know what lies beneath it.
Two digits follow the point, so lay the number over a hundred: 34/100.
Seek what 34 and 100 have in common — both are even, so halve each and see what remains before it can be reduced no further.
storyContext: By candlelight, Stevin marks a partial measure of 0.34 of a guilder in the paymaster's ledger, and the clerk insists it be entered as a proper fraction before the wages can be paid.

question:
Employees at a college help desk track the number of people who request assistance each week, as recorded below:

Compute the mode.
explanation: I go through the whole column and keep a tally beside each figure, for the mode is simply the number that appears most often. When I count 156, I find it written four separate times — 156, 156, 156, and 156 — while no other value reaches that many. So the value that repeats most is 156.
hints: The mode is not about averages or sums — it is only ever the value that shows itself most frequently. Fix your eye on repetition.
Begin by making a small mark each time a number appears, working steadily down the list so none escapes you.
Compare your tallies: several values like 153 and 148 appear twice, but one figure surpasses them all — find the number counted four times and name it.
storyContext: By lamplight Florence spreads the weekly ward tallies across her table, thirty numbers scrawled in different hands, and she needs to know which figure recurs more than any other before the pattern can speak.

question: A pharmacy offers students at a nearby college a discount. Jerry purchases ibuprofen, which had an original price of 15.80 dollars. The cost to Jerry after the student discount was 13.43 dollars. What is the percent discount for students at the pharmacy?
explanation: First I look at how much was taken away: 15.80 - 13.43 = 2.37, so the merchant saved 2.37. Then I ask what part of the whole that saving is, dividing 2.37 by the full price 15.80, which gives 0.15. A part written as 0.15 out of one is the same as 15 out of a hundred, so the discount is 15%.
hints: A discount is simply the piece of the price that vanished — find that piece first, then see how large it is compared to the whole.
Subtract what he paid from what it first cost: 15.80 - 13.43. That difference is the coin left in his purse.
Take that difference and divide it by the original 15.80; the number you get, turned into parts of a hundred, is the discount.
storyContext: As the priests debate, a merchant beside Thales grumbles over his scrolls of accounts, unable to name the fraction the pharmacy in Miletus took off his purchase of remedy priced at 15.80 that cost him only 13.43.

question: Use the following information to answer the next two exercises. A credit card company charges 10 dollars when a payment is late, and 5 dollars a day each day the payment remains unpaid. Find the equation that expresses the total fee in terms of the number of days the payments is late.
explanation: I look at it plain: there's the one-time charge of 10 the moment it's late, and that never changes. Then for every day it stays unpaid, another 5 piles on, so that's 5 times x days, or 5x. Add the fixed 10 to the growing 5x and the whole fee comes to y = 10 + 5x.
hints: Sort the charges into two kinds: one that happens once, and one that keeps adding with each passing day.
Start with the charge that never changes no matter how long the debt sits — that lone 10 that lands the instant it's late.
Now take the 5 charged every day and multiply it by x days to get 5x, then join it to the fixed charge with addition.
storyContext: Below deck by lamplight, William tallies the ship's ledger, where a lender back in Portsmouth has marked a penalty for every day my father's debt runs past its due.

question: Marisol needed to buy a new microwave. She got a 26% discount. The sale price Marisol paid was 43.66 dollars. What was the original price of the microwave?
explanation: See, when 26% is taken away, what remains is 100% − 26% = 74% of the first price, and that remaining part is the 43.66 he handed over. So 74% of some number gives 43.66, meaning that number times 0.74 equals 43.66. I turn it around: 43.66 divided by 0.74 gives back the whole, which is 59. And 59 times 0.74 returns 43.66, so the original price was 59.
hints: The price he paid is not the full price — the discount was already carved out. Think about what fraction of the whole still remains after 26% is gone.
If 26% was removed, then 100% − 26% = 74% is left, and that 74% is exactly the 43.66 he paid.
Write it as original × 0.74 = 43.66, so the original is 43.66 divided by 0.74 — do that division to uncover the first price.
storyContext: As the priests test him, a merchant nearby grumbles about a bazaar deal: he paid 43.66 for a bronze mirror marked down 26%, yet cannot recall its first price. Thales pauses, curious.

question: Calculate 2/5 ÷ 3/10 + 1/6 .
explanation: Dividing by 3/10 is the same as multiplying by its flip, so 2/5 becomes 2/5 × 10/3. The tops give 2 × 10 = 20 and the bottoms 5 × 3 = 15, which is 20/15, and that trims down to 4/3. Add the remaining 1/6 by matching denominators — 8/6 + 1/6 = 9/6 — and it settles at 3/2.
hints: A division of fractions is no beast to fear — turn the divisor on its head and the work becomes a plain multiplication, then deal with the lone addition after.
Rewrite 2/5 ÷ 3/10 as 2/5 × 10/3 before you touch the 1/6 at all.
Multiply across to get 20/15, reduce it, then bring both that and 1/6 to a shared denominator and add — the total is a single fraction just a whisker away.
storyContext: By candlelight, Stevin measures a share of grain against a smaller ration, then must fold in one more parcel before the paymaster's ledger will balance.

question: Calculate the markup for the given cost and markup percentage. Then calculate the retail price. Cost = 1,800.00 dollars; percent markup is 22%
explanation: Trace the path: Convert percent to decimal: 22% = 0.22. Multiply cost by markup rate: 1800.00 × 0.22. Compute: 1800 × 0.22 = 396.00. Markup = 396.00. Add markup to cost: 1800.00 + 396.00. That lands on Markup = 396.00, Retail price = 2196.00 — the answer.
hints: A markup is a portion of the cost added on top of it — first find that portion, then join it to the original price.
Turn the 22% into a decimal, 0.22, and multiply it by the cost of 1800 to see how much is added.
Once you have 1800 × 0.22 as the markup, set it beside the cost and add: 1800 + (that markup) will give the retail price.
storyContext: As the priests debate, a merchant beside Thales frets over his shipment of linen: it cost him 1,800 dollars, and he must add a 22% markup before selling in the market at dawn.

question:
Which of the following equations are linear?
y = 6 x + 8
y + 7 = 3 x
y – x = 8 x 2
4 y = 8
explanation: I look at each line for the shape of a straight path — every variable sitting at power one, with no squares and no variables multiplied together. y = 6x + 8 keeps x plain, so it holds; y + 7 = 3x is the same, just shifted about; but y - x = 8x^2 carries an x^2, which bends the line, so I cast it out. And 4y = 8 is simply y standing alone, plain as day. The straight ones are y = 6x + 8; y + 7 = 3x; 4y = 8.
hints: A straight measure never bends — hunt for any variable lifted above the first power or two variables tangled together.
Check the highest power on each x and y; if it stays at one and nothing's multiplied together, that line runs true.
Three of them keep every variable at power one, but one hides an x^2 — set that bent one aside and keep the rest.
storyContext: Below deck, William steadies the watch against the ship's roll and studies his father's notes, where four equations mark the true measures of steady motion apart from the crooked, curving ones.

question: Determine the percent markup based on the given cost and retail price. Round percentages to two decimal places. Cost = 120.00 dollars; retail price = 190.00 dollars
explanation: First I see how much the price rose above the cost: 190.00 − 120.00 = 70.00. Then I ask what fraction that gain is of the original cost, so I divide: 70.00 / 120.00 = 0.583333... To speak it as merchants do, I turn it into parts per hundred by multiplying by 100, giving 58.3333..., which rounds to 58.33%.
hints: Markup is measured against what the merchant first paid, so keep your eyes fixed on the cost as your foundation.
Begin by finding the gain itself: take the retail price and subtract the cost from it.
Now divide that gain by the cost, 70.00 / 120.00, then multiply the result by 100 and round to two places to name the percent.
storyContext: As evening falls, a merchant from the market corners Thales, needing to know how much his wares gained in worth: acquired for 120.00 and now sold to travelers for 190.00.

question:
Use the steam-and-leaf plot below to answer the questions. Round all answers to three decimal places.
